questions.question_text: A broker receives earnest money on a Thursday afternoon. By when must it be deposited?
questions.explanation: Wisconsin requires all trust funds to be deposited within 48 hours of receipt. If received before a holiday, 2 business days are allowed. In this case, the 48-hour deadline is Saturday.

questions.question_text: Within how many hours must trust funds be deposited in Wisconsin?
questions.explanation: All trust funds — including earnest money, security deposits, rent, and other client funds — must be deposited within 48 hours of receipt. If received before a holiday, 2 business days are allowed.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is considered a trust fund that must be deposited within 48 hours?
questions.correct_answer: Rent collected on behalf of a property owner
distractors: The broker's commission check at closing
A referral fee from another broker
An advertising reimbursement from a seller
questions.explanation: Trust funds include earnest money, security deposits, rent, and any other client funds held by the broker. Commission payments and referral fees are the broker's own funds and are not subject to trust account rules.

questions.question_text: A salesperson receives a security deposit from a tenant on Friday before a Monday holiday. When is the deposit deadline?
questions.explanation: When trust funds are received before a holiday, the 2-business-day rule applies instead of the strict 48-hour rule. With Friday receipt and Monday as a holiday, 2 business days would be Tuesday.

questions.question_text: Who is responsible for ensuring trust funds are deposited timely?
distractors: The salesperson who collected the funds
The title company handling the transaction
questions.explanation: While salespersons must turn over trust funds to the broker promptly, it is the designated broker's responsibility to ensure all trust funds are deposited within the required 48-hour timeframe.

questions.question_text: Where does the interest earned on Wisconsin's IBRETA accounts go?
questions.correct_answer: Department of Administration for homeless assistance programs
distractors: Back to the client whose funds earned the interest
To the broker as an administrative fee
To the REEB for licensing administration
questions.explanation: IBRETA interest goes to the Department of Administration, which distributes it for emergency shelter and transitional homeless programs. The interest does not go to the broker or the client.

questions.question_text: Who is the beneficial owner of interest earned on an IBRETA account?
distractors: The broker who maintains the account
The client whose funds generated the interest
questions.explanation: Under Wisconsin law, the Department of Administration is the beneficial owner of IBRETA interest. Brokers may never remove or use the interest earned on these accounts. The funds support homeless assistance programs.

questions.question_text: Can a broker use the interest earned on their IBRETA account to cover business expenses?
questions.correct_answer: No, the broker may never remove or use IBRETA interest
distractors: Yes, up to $500 per year for administrative costs
Yes, if approved by DSPS in advance
Yes, after the annual remittance to the state
questions.explanation: At no time may the firm remove or use the interest earned on IBRETA accounts. The interest belongs to the Department of Administration for homeless assistance programs — not the broker or clients.

questions.question_text: What must a firm do within 10 days of changing their trust account to a different bank?
questions.correct_answer: Notify DSPS of the account change
distractors: Obtain REEB approval for the new bank
Transfer the interest to the old bank
questions.explanation: Firms must notify DSPS within 10 days of any trust account changes, including changing depository institutions, changing account names or numbers, or closing a trust account.

questions.question_text: What is unique about Wisconsin's IBRETA requirement compared to standard trust accounts?
questions.correct_answer: Interest must be used for homeless assistance programs
distractors: Brokers can keep the interest as income
Clients receive all interest earned on their funds
Interest is split between the broker and DSPS
questions.explanation: IBRETA is unique to Wisconsin. Unlike regular trust accounts, the interest earned on pooled client funds is directed to the Department of Administration specifically for homeless emergency shelter and transitional housing programs.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits a client's earnest money into the firm's operating account. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Commingling is mixing client trust funds with the broker's personal or business operating funds. Trust funds must be kept in a separate trust account, never in the firm's operating account.

questions.question_text: How often can REEB audit a broker's trust account?
questions.explanation: REEB has the authority to audit trust accounts at any time. Brokers must maintain proper records and be prepared for an audit without advance notice.

questions.question_text: A licensee wants to use a non-standard escrow arrangement for earnest money. Who may draft this agreement?
questions.correct_answer: The parties or an attorney, not the licensee
distractors: The designated broker of the firm
questions.explanation: Alternative escrow arrangements must be drafted by the parties themselves or by an attorney. Licensees cannot draft custom escrow agreements, as this constitutes unauthorized practice of law.

questions.question_text: What records must a broker maintain regarding trust accounts?
questions.correct_answer: Complete accounting records available for REEB inspection
distractors: Only annual balance summaries for DSPS
Records for the current year only
questions.explanation: Brokers must maintain complete trust account records including proper accounting and documentation. These records must be available for REEB inspection at any time.

questions.question_text: Under REEB 18, how much personal money may a firm keep in a trust account to cover service charges?
distractors: No personal funds are ever permitted
questions.explanation: REEB 18.10 allows firms to deposit and maintain up to $300 of personal funds in the trust account specifically to cover bank service charges. This is the only exception to the commingling prohibition.
